sections.title: Global Equities
sections.content: A semiconductor-led rout dominated as investors questioned stretched AI valuations and the US-Iran standoff escalated, reversing the prior week's tech leadership. MSCI World fell roughly **-1.5%** (approx., verify before merging), with MSCI Developed Markets near **-1.5%** (approx., verify before merging) and MSCI Emerging Markets around **-2%** (approx., verify before merging). The S&P 500 lost **1.6%** to about **7,458** and the tech-heavy Nasdaq slid **2.9%**, as the MAG7 mega-cap cohort dropped sharply (approx., verify before merging) amid a near-**9%** weekly fall in the VanEck Semiconductor ETF. Europe was mixed, with the Euro Stoxx 50 down about **1.6%** while the STOXX 600 held broadly flat at **+0.07%**. China's CSI 300 fell roughly **2.5%** (approx., verify before merging), Hong Kong's Hang Seng around **2%** (approx., verify before merging) and Japan's Nikkei 225 tumbled **6.4%** to about **64,140**.

sections.title: US Equities
sections.content:
US equities retreated, sharply reversing the previous week's mega-cap technology leadership. The S&P 500 fell **1.6%** to about **7,458**, the Nasdaq Composite slid **2.9%** to **25,520** and the Dow Jones Industrial Average eased **0.9%** to **52,146**, with information technology and communication services posting the steepest sector losses.

A deep semiconductor rout drove the decline: the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index fell about **10%** on the week as investors questioned whether AI-linked valuations had run ahead of fundamentals. Sentiment soured further on the debut of a competitive AI model from Chinese startup Moonshot and reports of a delay to Alphabet's next Gemini release, while energy shares advanced alongside a near-**4%** rise in oil to about **$81**.

Macro data leaned supportive, with June CPI posting its largest monthly decline in more than six years, reinforcing a disinflation narrative and expectations that the Fed holds rates steady through year-end even as geopolitics clouded the outlook.

sections.title: European Equities
sections.content:
European equities ended a volatile week broadly unchanged as Friday's technology weakness in the US and Asia spread across the region. The pan-European STOXX Europe 600 rose just **0.07%** in local-currency terms, masking notable dispersion beneath the surface.

The UK's FTSE 100, with relatively little technology exposure, climbed **0.98%**, while Germany's DAX fell **0.94%**, France's CAC 40 was roughly flat and Italy's FTSE MIB dropped **1.39%**. The Euro Stoxx 50 shed about **1.6%** as chip and AI-related names tracked global peers lower, though energy and defensive sectors offered some cushion.

Higher oil rekindled inflation concerns and kept the rates backdrop in focus, reinforcing a defensive tone and leaving cyclical, tech-heavy bourses lagging their more value-oriented regional peers.

sections.title: APAC Equities
sections.content:
Asian markets were hit hard by the global semiconductor selloff. Japan's Nikkei 225 tumbled about **6.4%** to roughly **64,140**, one of its worst weeks of the year, as heavyweight chip and AI-linked shares slid on valuation concerns and profit-taking after recent strong gains.

Chinese equities also fell, with the mainland CSI 300 down around **2.5%** and Hong Kong's Hang Seng near **2%** lower. Second-quarter GDP grew **4.3%** year over year, below the **4.5%** consensus and down from **5.0%** in the first quarter, though June industrial production rose a firmer-than-expected **5.3%** and retail sales returned to growth at **1.0%**.

Trade data surprised strongly to the upside, with exports up **27.0%** year over year and the trade surplus widening to about **$125.6bn**, even as the launch of competitive domestic AI models underscored an intensifying regional tech cycle.

sections.title: Foreign Exchange
sections.content:
Currency markets increasingly took their cue from interest-rate differentials rather than geopolitics, with the US dollar broadly firm on higher-for-longer Fed expectations, resilient growth and a widening yield advantage across the G10.

Sterling had another constructive week, with GBP/USD rallying to its highest level since mid-May and GBP/EUR breaching **1.18** to a fresh one-year high, though overbought momentum and narrowing front-end spreads suggested the move versus the euro had become stretched.

The yen stayed under pressure, with USD/JPY holding just below its multi-decade peak of **162.84** near four-decade lows, weighed by higher oil, fiscal concerns and renewed Middle East tension, keeping intervention risk elevated as the yield gap remained wide.

sections.title: Commodities
sections.content:
Energy reasserted itself as the dominant cross-asset driver. Oil rose about **4%** to roughly **$81**, with Brent topping **$85**, after the US reinstated a blockade of Iranian ships in the Strait of Hormuz and demanded a **20%** levy on transiting cargo, reviving supply-disruption and inflation fears while backwardation flattered returns.

Precious metals remained among 2026's weakest performers, with the Bloomberg Precious Metals Index down about **9.5%** year to date and gold off roughly **8.2%**. Higher fuel costs kept inflation concerns alive and raised the risk that central banks stay restrictive, weighing on non-yielding assets as gold and silver struggled for direction.

The higher-oil, sticky-inflation mix tightened financial conditions and sharpened the divergence between a resilient energy complex and a soft metals sector, feeding back into equity and rates volatility.

sections.title: Fixed Income
sections.content:
Sovereign yields drifted lower as the technology selloff and escalating Middle East tensions drove a modest flight to safety. The 10-year UST yield fell more than **4bps** to about **4.525%**, the 2-year eased **3bps** to roughly **4.124%** and the 30-year slipped to around **5.061%**.

June CPI's largest monthly decline in over six years reinforced a benign inflation backdrop, and Edward Jones reiterated its view that the Fed is likely to keep rates on hold for the remainder of 2026, with contained inflation and a stable labour market keeping the bar for hikes high.

Credit tone stayed broadly steady despite the equity wobble, with IG resilient and HY supported by still-healthy risk appetite, though elevated valuations and geopolitical uncertainty argued for continued selectivity and an emphasis on quality.
